name: Abuse of Stolen Session Tokens in SaaS
fullName: Abuse of Stolen Session Tokens in SaaS
severity: High to Critical (based on data access and privilege level)
detection: CASB, SIEM, EDR, SaaS Audit Logs, User Reports, Threat Intelligence
scenario: An attacker gains access to a valid session token (e.g., via XSS, phishing, malware or token theft from endpoints) and uses it to impersonate a legitimate user on a SaaS platform (e.g., Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce, Slack). This allows access without triggering MFA or login anomaly alerts.
tools: CASB (e.g., Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps, Netskope, Lookout); SaaS Security Posture Management (e.g., Obsidian, AppOmni); SIEM (e.g., Sentinel, Splunk); Endpoint Detection and Response (e.g., CrowdStrike, Cortex XDR); Identity Providers (e.g., Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace); Browser Security

detSteps.title: Detect session token reuse from unexpected IP or device
detSteps.detail: Look for the same session token being used from a new IP, new geographic location, or new user-agent string — without a corresponding new authentication event. This indicates the token was stolen and is being replayed by an attacker on a different device.

queries.splunk: index=cloud sourcetype=saas_audit session_id=[suspect_session] | stats dc(src_ip) as ip_count values(src_ip) as sources, values(user_agent) as agents | where ip_count > 1

detSteps.title: Review all actions taken with the stolen session
detSteps.detail: Document every action performed using the stolen session token: files accessed, messages sent, settings changed, data exported, new OAuth grants made. This determines the scope of the breach and any data exposure.

queries.splunk: index=cloud sourcetype=o365 OR sourcetype=gws session_id=[suspect_session] | stats count by Operation, resource | sort _time
queries.velociraptor: SELECT Pid, Name, CommandLine, Exe, CreateTime FROM pslist() ORDER BY CreateTime DESC LIMIT 150

contSteps.title: Force logout and revoke all active sessions for the affected user
contSteps.detail: Revoke all active sessions across all devices for the compromised account. In M365: Revoke-AzureADUserAllRefreshToken. In Google Workspace: Admin Console > Users > Reset Sign-in Cookies. This immediately terminates the attacker's access.

eradSteps.title: Scan endpoint for malware: Ensure no token stealer is still active on the user’s device
Rotate any exposed credentials or tokens: For linked applications or integrations
Review session storage practices: Ensure session tokens are not stored in plaintext or improperly cached
Strengthen SaaS login policies: Enforce re-authentication for sensitive actions or high-risk logins

recSteps.title: Re-enable user access with strict monitoring: After ensuring the endpoint is clean and MFA is re-enforced
Monitor user activity closely: Apply alerts on behavioural deviation or abnormal downloads
Educate user on signs of session hijacking: Reinforce best practices for session security
Conduct internal checks: To ensure no lateral movement or privilege abuse occurred
